speaker: interviewer
text: Roll [? four, ?] Maurice Markheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: this janitor invited you to stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, to live there. She give me food. She give me anything I want. She fixed me some clothes up. Then later, little by little, there was a Jewish community over there. And when you were going there, you could get-- I think they give you some money. You got food. Because there were Jewish people there. It's community. You know, it's like here, you know-- Memorial-- not Memorial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Martyrs Memorial?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Federation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and every day they used to hang up a list, from differents came from all over Europe. Who survive? Who survive? Who else survive? And that's the way I find out who survive, who didn't survive. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find someone? Did you find your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I find-- I find one, especially one-- one of my brother, I found out, but he couldn't-- he couldn't-- he wasn't in the shape to be transported from Mauthausen to-- to-- to-- to-- to Cracow. He was all swollen from-- from hunger. He was all swelled up, all over. If you punch-- if you touch his body, if you punch a finger and you-- you-- remained a hole. But after six weeks he was OK. You know, they brought him up. And I stay, I live there a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I start to do a little bit more black market. [? So ?] [? she ?] [? told ?] [? me, ?] [? what, ?] [? aren't ?] [? you ?] [? a ?] [? brother? ?] So I did it. I did this, that favor; this, that favor. You know? I was-- I was supporting myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I have to go-- then I had my brother come back. And he said no, don't live here. That my-- we have a cousin. He has now, you know, an apartment. We're going to go live with the cousin. I didn't want it, really. But I-- but I did. But eventually it comes-- what comes-- I think this was Rosh Hashanah. You know, next year already. Whatever it is, there was a temple there. There was one temple was open. I was confused. I didn't believe anymore. But I said, ah, I'm going to go. And here where the Polacks-- that's the Polish people-- start a pogrom on the Jews. They toss-- start to throw the rock in the synagogue. And they start to scream that the Jews who survived the war, they have a Polish child there, they have to slaughter the child for sacrificing for some, [? like ?] [? that. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was pretty dangerous, you know? And this was in such a neighborhood. It was all very bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what to do. I got scared. All of a sudden, from far away, I see a-- a-- a friend of mine. He was in the Polish uniform. He was in the police. But he was in the-- the other army, the Communist Army. He came with the Communist Army to Cracow. And we were-- we were friendly before the war. But here, he had already enough. It's all of a sudden. And under the gun, he took me out like that. He said, you walk forward. And I'm going to stay behind you with a gun. And I take you all the way to the [? circa. ?] And you go to the-- to the center of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they kill many people that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know that yeah, there were-- there were some. There were some. I don't know how many there. I can't tell you, because I got shook up. I got scared. And-- and I left Poland. I smuggle myself to Linz, out of Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the last time. But you know, how many? In 1981 or '82, I went back, you know, to fresh my memory. Nothing's changed. That's the same.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't believe they ever will change. They got the lessons, too, don't get me wrong. But you know with-- it's like a-- that antisemitism, that hate for the Jews, the jealousy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? It's only plain jealousy. I-- I-- I think when-- when-- when the mother feed the baby through the milk, the anti-- antisemitism go into the baby. Antisemitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Antisemitism go into baby. Because-- but the next-- I believe now, this generation is a little bit better already. They change already. They-- they-- they realize it. Because the other day we had affair in [? 39 ?] [? Club. ?] The big Polish consulate was there. They brought him over to the table. They brought him over to the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the way he says-- the way he was talking, you know, a young fellow, that they are complete different people. Let's hope so. So it--

speaker: interviewee
text: But a man my age, thank God, I'm retired. I'm successful. I have a nice family.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm an American. I got a wonderful wife. I'm happy. What else I can ask? Just pray to God to be healthy, and that's all. And go back to God. How you turn around? You can't sneak around. No? Would you believe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And believe you me, the story I told you, it's-- I will say, this just briefly. Because it's impossible to have it so organized. It's just that-- there's nothing but-- what I could say now, more. I'm sure there is much more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll-- if you ask me, maybe I will answer you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I would like to ask you, what you would say to your great-great grandchildren, if you could, in 200 years? What would you say to them? What message do you have for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a message. Stay strong and have leadership. Let this leadership lead you. Because believe you me, how young I was. And there were younger people. There were younger kids than me. If we would have a leadership-- I'm talking about 1939, at that time-- and if they will say, here is a gun, go and kill.

speaker: interviewer
text: We probably would do it. I would do it. You see? That's all I can tell my children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Remember, have a leadership. Be together. Watch-- watch what's going on in the world. Get involved, a little bit in everything. Even if it's sometimes on your own-- on your own expense, it's well worth it. Educate them. Because with education you can do so much that is unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you believe that-- I couldn't believe it-- that Hitler open up a special school to train the people to get into the SS, and to train them to be-- to stay-- to-- to-- to-- to kill the Jews. Mass-- mass-- mass killing.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a special school for it, to train them there. Say for example, the Ukrainians. Well, this I know as a fact. They-- they were against-- against-- no, against the Russian, against the communism, against Stalin. They joined the-- the-- the SS. They joined the Hitler army. Before he got into the army, they send them to the school. How to take care of the Jews, what to think about the Jews, how to kill them, how to torture them. How. First, psychologically. Then automatically, [? they ?] will go by [? themselves. ?] You see, for that you have to have education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you tell me a man-- I can't visualize today even-- who was doing the mass killing, he was standing on the machine gun. And he was, all the time, drunk and supplied with-- with liquor. You know? --how today, he can live with himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'll give you a small example. About 10 years ago, maybe. Well, I changed. I got older 10 years. I went to Grand Canyon. Beautiful place, marvelous place. And all of a sudden-- then we stay in a beautiful hotel there. And all of us sudden we're sitting and eating-- and eating dinner. And-- a-- a bus with tourists pulled over. They were tourists. They sit next to us. You know, they spread them around the table. They start to speak German. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I tell you the truth. If not my friend, I don't know what would happen. I couldn't control myself. I-- It's true, I had a glass of wine. But when I hear that language and I saw that faces, God knows his father or his grandfather-- younger-- younger people, that his grandfather wasn't the killer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this got into me. So we left. We went to the-- to the-- the room and I went to sleep. You know? Because sometimes you never know how you're going to react. How did you react in your life that's different than you would have been because of the war? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: How I would have act?

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you react--

speaker: interviewee
text: Today?

speaker: interviewer
text: --these days, after the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Today?

speaker: interviewer
text: --that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, listen--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that is-- that is a result of the things that happened to [? you when they did? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen, you have to live. You have to forgive. I got more educated.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to forgive, but you cannot forget. There is no medication, what will heal your computer to forget. You forgive. Because most of those people, maybe they're still alive, so they're old like I am, you see? But you can't forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: And believe you me, if we get together, and I guarantee you for any occasion, you name it-- you leave-- when we leave there is always talk, the end of it, the conversation is about our past between us. That is any get together that we wouldn't talk about. I'm talking just privately, not in a club, in a public place or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Between each other. This-- this reminds them of a story. This remind-- and we know each other for the 40 year-- for 40, 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maurice, how are you a different person because of the war? What did the war do to your personality?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today?

speaker: interviewer
text: Since the war. Did you bring up your children differently because of the war? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a personal relationships--

speaker: interviewee
text: With my children, I'll tell you the truth. I couldn't go too far. I'll be honest with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? They listen in the beginning. The disturbing at night, the nightmare what I had, running away from the SS, screaming, hollering, they don't want it. They said, dad that's enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: If there was a movie about the war on television, they went and said, turn it off. It's the same thing when I was sitting smoking that cigarette. They took the cigarette away from my hand. Dad, no. That's no good for you. They wouldn't-- they wouldn't-- I mean, in other words, the case was closed for them. But always, try to give them a better life, what I had. And that they never have to come through what I went through. But if you have to be, organize and have a leadership. Stick together. Because no matter what it is, we're the minority. You know, I never was a soldier. I never thought of-- of that, anything, any-- you know, in that respect. I never should have gone. Never, in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you wish you would have been more powerful?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: I hear you. Maurice, what about your children? You say that they didn't want you to think about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they--

speaker: interviewee
text: So they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Have they changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: They listen. Oh, yeah. In the beginning, they listen. Oh yeah, they know. They know what I went through. No question about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you know, each time, it's like another version. I was a dedicated father. you see? I spent a lot of time with my children, you see? All I-- All I knew at that time, and all that I want to make a living, an honest living, and be with my family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- when my first wife divorce me, I was-- they were about 17. They were teenagers already. So I couldn't control them anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: But thank God, I have wonderful children. I know they love me. I know they care about me. You see?

speaker: interviewee
text: They won't listen to me. They have their own life. But at least whatever I could put in their mind, they have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: One granddaughter. She's only two years old. She is only two years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure you'll have more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. I-- I hope so. That's all what we have to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to introduce your wife now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. How about when you want to-- I got one picture I want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we'll show the pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Can you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you introduce your wife? Tell us her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Got her, ?] [? the ?] [? girl. ?] This is my lovely wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regina. I call her [? Lana ?] Markheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Lana ?] Markheim. And how long have you two been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 18, 19 years, approximately, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: A long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: A long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. And you said, [? Lana, ?] are you from Poland as well? REGINA (WIFE): Yes, I am. And how did you survive the war? REGINA (WIFE): Well, I've been in ghettos. We went through hell. And then from the ghetto, I was on the Aryan side. In Warsaw? REGINA (WIFE): In Warsaw, and other places also. What other places? Small-- REGINA (WIFE): In Germany. In Germany? REGINA (WIFE): Mhm. I see. I asked your husband a question, and I didn't get a full answer. How is he different, do you think, because of his war experiences? REGINA (WIFE): Well, I don't know if he's different than anybody else, I guess. I didn't know him before the war. That's right. REGINA (WIFE): So it's hard to say. But that's all I can tell you. Do you recognize certain characteristics in him that you believe are because of the war? Does he have bad dreams? REGINA (WIFE): Sometimes he cries-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --while sleeping. But other than that, I don't know. And when you cry when sleeping, it has to do with you running away, huh? REGINA (WIFE): Probably, yes. But then I do the same thing, I guess. Always running and running, because that's what I used to do during the war. So those are the dreams you have? REGINA (WIFE): Mhm. Both of you, running dreams? REGINA (WIFE): Yeah. Did you also go back to Poland after the war? REGINA (WIFE): I was-- yes, I was once in Poland. And how was that for you? Did you find places and-- REGINA (WIFE): Very-- well, I went-- mainly I went to my town, the little shtetl that I was born. And it was very emotional, very emotional. What was the name of that shtetl? REGINA (WIFE): [POLISH] And what was your name then? REGINA (WIFE): Litmanowicz. Thank you. Well, I was just hearing that you were there, very much, when Maurice had his surgery. REGINA (WIFE): Yes. Maurice, what were you saying about how your wife took care of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could think only afterward, you see? I have to give her all the credit, nothing but credit. And I think I did something good in my life. Because God took care of me, too. And now I-- I go to the-- to the synagogue, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, with my wife. And we observe. My wife have a kosher home.

speaker: interviewer
text: I believe in it. I enjoy it. She's a great woman. So you've forgiven God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're friends again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Maurice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, [? Lana. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you too. REGINA (WIFE): Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to see the photographs now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, just a moment. This-- This is Estera Markheim. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Picture taken in ghetto. This is approximately 1941 or '42. She doesn't look so good here, anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Cracow? It's your--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your mother right?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my-- that's my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. What, about 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: '40, approximately. '40 or '41. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next picture I'm showing is my older sister. Her name is Regina Markheim. And she is born in Cracow. And the date of the picture is from before the war. Was-- could be '37, '38. I don't know exactly. Oh yeah. Next to it is an empty space dedicated for my brother, Natan. I never could find another-- get no picture of him. Next to him on the left side, on the bottom, is Michael Markheim, my brother. And next to him is John Markheim, my brother. And the name, naturally, is all Markheim. Then the next one in the circle, that's me right after the war, maybe six months after the war, five months, in Cracow. Next to that, that is my older brother, Dave, Dave Markheim. It's after the war, the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was taken in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Cracow. It could be in 1946. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is taken in Cracow. It's already during the war. On that day, my sister on the left, Regina, she find out that her husband, end of the war, committed suicide. Next to her to the right is my older sister, Amalia. And down below them is them is-- the-- the is my nephew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your nephew survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody survive from them. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my brother-in-law, who committed suicide with her son, swimming and teaching my-- my nephew how to swim in Park Roztocze, in the swimming pool. And this picture has been taken in 19-- probably 1938. Next to it is a-- the place is Cracow, and Cracow Park Roztocze. And the date is approximately 1938. And his last name, my-- my-- my brother that's-- my brother-- brother-in-law's name was Schnuer.

speaker: interviewer
text: First name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sameh. Sam Schnuer. Romek Schnuer. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The next-- the next picture is my first-- from the left on the top, is my first cousin. His name is-- the place? Oh. The-- his name is Romek Nussbaum.

speaker: interviewee
text: The place is taking right on the Vistula, in the summertime. You can see the background of the world-famous castle, Wawel. Wawel, they called it. Next to him, that's me. I-- I-- I don't remember. I can't tell you what exactly. Maybe 1934, 1935, that picture. All-- all the people here, my-- the family-- the names is-- is-- most of them is forgotten. I know only Denik Fuchs, which I can point-- point out him. My-- my uncle-- my brother, Dave, is there. Well, let me see closely. The first one, the-- the second in the back in the second row, that's a gentile fellow. That's a friend of my brother. Next to him is Dave, my brother. Next to Dave is Leon Fuchs, a friend of my brother. There's another gentile fellow on the bottom in the front, another gentile fellow. The second in the middle is his-- is-- is Fuch's brother, Denik Fuchs. He was hanged in Plaszow. And I had to watch him, the way Goth hang him. And the other fellow is another-- is another gentile fellow. It's-- it's-- it's hard to remember. I'm too upset to describe you just specifically every one. But that's a valuable picture to me. Because the picture had been taken maybe, in 1935, 1936. Because the most tragic thing in that picture is the way they hanged Denik Fuchs. Which the guy is from the right-- second from the right, on the very bottom. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is showing wedding of my sister. My brother-in-law's name is Sam Schnuer. REGINA (WIFE): Was. My sister is Regina Markheim. It's taken in Cracow-- I can't remember-- 1933, '34. I can't remember. Maybe '35. This is wedding of my sister. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Below this picture is their son, Romek. Also taken just-- too, it's just before the war. It could be 1938. He was maybe about four or five years old at that time. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In this picture, on the very-- on the left, you see a Polish policemen escorting Amon Goth, which is right the guy next to him. He was the-- the most brutal camp commander in Plaszow. This picture is taken place in Cracow. I took that personally. But this is by the end. I think this is in the-- in 1946, in the beginning of 1946. He's here, escorted to-- to court for-- for his trial. And a few weeks later, he was hanged in Plaszow, in the place where he commit the mass murder. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] In this picture is [? Jack. ?] His name is [? Jack ?] [? Rose. ?] He is my stepson. What a great guy. And his wife is [? Helen ?] [? Stan. ?] She's a good wife, too. But then one of the greatest is Jordan. He's six months old. That's his son.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Do they live here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they live in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date of the photo was 1994?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is Steven Markheim and his wife, and his daughter, baby. They live in Minneapolis. They live happy. Thank God. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Picture, this is taken in 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What is-- what is the baby's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alex-- oh, yeah. I forgot. That's the most important thing. Alexandra. REGINA (WIFE): (WHISPERING) Alexandra.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us more about it. What did you say the wife's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Debbie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Debbie. And they live where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Minneapolis.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Minneapolis? And as you said, they live happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1994. REGINA (WIFE): '93.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name-- this is my daughter, Reina. Her name is also, well, now, she was Markheim. She's still single. That picture is taken in 1994 in Turkey, not in-- not in California, where she lives. And she tried to makes her life there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she's a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Still single.

speaker: interviewer
text: --school teacher, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she's a teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she loves to ski.

speaker: interviewee
text: Teach.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's why she lives up there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taking after her father, with sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could-- I wish she would think like the father. [LAUGHTER]
